Client 2: Is that what hits the bank? Because that’s… not that high.
Advisor: Good question—no, not what hits the bank. Before taxes. If we did it the “hits the bank” way, it’d be net. Gross is the bigger number.
Client 1: Gross for the household is about $224,600 a year.
Client 2: Yeah, that sounds right when you say gross.
Advisor: Great—$224,600 gross annually. And just so I’m not mixing who’s who: Margaret, you’re Client 1, and Thomas, you’re Client 2—correct?

Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to that “recurring payments” point, I want to map your baseline monthly outflow first. About how much do you spend per month all-in—housing, food, insurance, everything?
Client 2: Uh, like six thousand-ish? Maybe a little more.
Client 1: It’s higher. It’s about $6,850 a month—pretty steady.
Advisor: Got it—$6,850 per month. Thanks, Margaret. Thomas, when you said “six thousand-ish,” were you thinking just the bills, not the whole spend?
Client 2: Yeah—sorry, I was thinking like utilities and groceries, not the whole thing.

Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to that “recurring payments” point, I want to map your baseline monthly outflow first. About how much do you spend per month all-in—housing, food, insurance, everything?
Client 2: Uh, like six thousand-ish? Maybe a little more.
Client 1: It’s higher. It’s about $6,850 a month—pretty steady.
Advisor: Got it—$6,850 per month. Thanks, Margaret. Thomas, when you said “six thousand-ish,” were you thinking just the bills, not the whole spend?
Client 2: Yeah—sorry, I was thinking like utilities and groceries, not the whole thing.

Advisor: Let’s switch to assets—investable assets first, then the house. Roughly, what do you have across investment accounts and cash-like accounts, excluding the home value?
Client 2: A million? Like around a million.
Client 1: It’s $1,036,900 total investable—so, about $1.04 million.
Advisor: Okay—$1,036,900, about $1.04 million. Let’s break that into the buckets you have so we don’t miss anything.
Advisor: Thomas, I’m seeing a taxable account that’s held with an insurance company—does that ring a bell? About $713,650?

Advisor: And you also have a private markets holding at a bank—about $46,050?
Client 1: Correct—$46,050.
Advisor: Then the home: primary residence held at a bank—value around $694,200. That’s the house value you’d use, roughly?
Client 2: Yeah, around $694k.
Client 1: $694,200 is the number we’ve been using.

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi Thomas—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you two?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, clarity—like, what we can spend and still hit goals, and how the second-marriage stuff fits without drama.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… I don’t want surprises. The recurring payments are what stress me out.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Going back to that “recurring payments” point, I want to map your baseline monthly outflow first. About how much do you spend per month all-in—housing, food, insurance, everything?

Client 1: Yes—two adults, no dependents.
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—writing that down.
Advisor: Next one’s taxes. When we talked earlier about income and the investment accounts, I had you in the 37% federal bracket—does that sound right, or am I off?
Client 2: Is that like… the amount we pay total? Because it doesn’t feel like 37% comes out.
Advisor: Good question—no, not what you pay overall. That’s the marginal bracket, the rate on the next chunk of income. Does 37% as your top bracket sound right, Margaret?

Client 2: Yeah, around $694k.
Client 1: $694,200 is the number we’ve been using.
Advisor: Perfect. And you said earlier—no mortgage, no other loans. So liabilities are basically none, correct?
Client 2: None. No credit cards carrying balances either.
Advisor: Good. Now protection—do you have any long-term care coverage in place?

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi Thomas—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you two?
Client 1: Hi Alex. Honestly, clarity—like, what we can spend and still hit goals, and how the second-marriage stuff fits without drama.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… I don’t want surprises. The recurring payments are what stress me out.

Client 2: Uh, like six thousand-ish? Maybe a little more.

Client 2: Oh—okay, yeah. US RIA then.
Client 1: Yes, that’s right—US RIA.
Advisor: Perfect, thanks. Next—marital status. You two are married to each other now, and it’s a second marriage situation—secondly wedded. Is that the right way to describe it?
Client 2: Yeah, second marriage for both of us.
Client 1: Right—secondly wedded, unfortunately the cleanest label.

Client 2: Yeah. And—sorry—just to be clear, it’s not like we’re hiding it, it’s just… touchy.
Advisor: Totally get it. We’ll keep it matter-of-fact.
Advisor: Last one: risk tolerance. Earlier you both sounded growth-leaning but with guardrails—cash buffer, obligations covered. Would you call the household risk tolerance “growth” overall?
Client 2: I mean—I'm cautious. I don’t love big drops.
Client 1: But we did agree growth, Thomas—because we’ve got the pension and the buffer.

Advisor: Thanks. Now I want to make sure we’ve captured the income sources correctly. Margaret, you have employment income—about $138,950 gross, right?
Client 1: Yes, that’s my salary—about $138,950.
Advisor: And Thomas, you’re not working right now, but there’s still income on your side—about $85,650 gross annually?
Client 2: Yeah, that’s right. Not wages, but that’s the number.
Advisor: Okay. And then, going back to what you said earlier about recurring payments—there’s also monthly Social Security and a pension coming in, both tied to you, Margaret. Does that sound right?

Advisor: Great—thanks for clearing that up. One sec—let me note that down.
Advisor: Next, Thomas, I want to confirm your birthday—month, day, year.
Client 2: November 12th, 1972.
Advisor: November 12, 1972—thank you. Margaret, does that match what you have?
Client 1: Yes, that’s right.

Client 2: Uh, like six thousand-ish? Maybe a little more.

Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I hear you, Margaret. Okay, going back to what you said earlier about the alimony being the touchy part, I want to make sure we’ve got Thomas’s side of the profile clean so the cash flow math isn’t off.
Advisor: Thomas, can I confirm your work status right now—are you currently working, or not working at the moment?
Client 2: Not working right now. I’m basically not employed—inactive, I guess is the word.
Advisor: Got it—currently not working. And just so I don’t confuse “not working” with “retired,” you’re not drawing wages from a job right now, correct?
Client 2: Correct. No paycheck.

Client 1: No—gross is before taxes. Net is what hits the bank.
Advisor: Exactly—thanks for catching that, Margaret. So $138,950 is pre-tax gross.

Client 1: Spouse is fine, yes.
